codeSnapshot.label: graphsage_node_classification.py
codeSnapshot.code:
import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F
from torch_geometric.nn import SAGEConv
from torch_geometric.datasets import Planetoid
from torch_geometric.loader import NeighborLoader

dataset = Planetoid(root='/tmp/Cora', name='Cora')
data    = dataset[0]

class GraphSAGE(torch.nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, in_channels, hidden, out_channels):
        super().__init__()
        self.conv1 = SAGEConv(in_channels, hidden, aggr='mean')
        self.conv2 = SAGEConv(hidden, out_channels, aggr='mean')

    def forward(self, x, edge_index):
        x = F.relu(self.conv1(x, edge_index))
        x = F.dropout(x, p=0.5, training=self.training)
        return self.conv2(x, edge_index)

# Mini-batch training with neighbourhood sampling
train_loader = NeighborLoader(
    data,
    num_neighbors=[10, 10],   # sample 10 neighbours per layer
    batch_size=64,
    input_nodes=data.train_mask,
)

device = torch.device('cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')
model  = GraphSAGE(dataset.num_features, 64, dataset.num_classes).to(device)
optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=0.005)

for epoch in range(10):
    model.train()
    for batch in train_loader:
        batch = batch.to(device)
        optimizer.zero_grad()
        out  = model(batch.x, batch.edge_index)
        loss = F.cross_entropy(out[:batch.batch_size], batch.y[:batch.batch_size])
        loss.backward(); optimizer.step()

# Inductive test: evaluate on full graph (including unseen nodes)
model.eval()
with torch.no_grad():
    out  = model(data.x.to(device), data.edge_index.to(device))
    pred = out.argmax(dim=1)
    acc  = (pred[data.test_mask.to(device)] == data.y.to(device)[data.test_mask]).float().mean()
print(f"Test accuracy: {acc.item():.4f}")  # ~0.81

eli3: GCN and GAT require the full graph to be in memory during training — they can't handle new nodes that weren't there during training. GraphSAGE (Graph SAmple and aggreGatE) fixes this by learning how to aggregate information from a sampled neighbourhood rather than from all neighbours at once.

The 'sample' part: instead of using all of a node's neighbours, GraphSAGE randomly picks a fixed number (e.g., 10 per layer). This makes training scalable — you never need the entire graph, just small subgraphs. The 'aggregate' part: take the sampled neighbour features, aggregate them (mean, max, LSTM), then concatenate with the node's own features.

The key insight: the aggregation function is what's learned, not the embeddings. This means GraphSAGE can generate embeddings for nodes it has never seen before — as long as the node has features and neighbours, you just run the aggregation. This is called inductive learning, and it's what makes GraphSAGE work in real-world systems like Pinterest where new users join every day.

whenWhy.title: Inductive node classification on evolving graphs
whenWhy.body: GCN and GAT are transductive — they require all nodes to be present during training and cannot generalise to new nodes. GraphSAGE is inductive: it learns an aggregation function applicable to any node with features, including nodes added after training. Hamilton et al. demonstrated this on protein interaction networks (PPI): train on 20 protein graphs, test on 2 unseen graphs. GraphSAGE achieves 0.768 F1 on unseen graphs; node2vec (transductive) achieves 0.151. This inductive capability is essential for dynamic graphs (social networks, web graphs) where new nodes appear continuously.

whenWhy.title: Large-scale industrial recommendation systems
whenWhy.body: Pinterest deployed PinSage, a GraphSAGE variant, on a bipartite graph of 3B pins and 18B edges — orders of magnitude larger than any transductive GNN can handle. PinSage uses importance sampling (weighted neighbourhood sampling based on random walk visit frequencies) rather than uniform sampling, and runs multi-GPU distributed training. GraphSAGE's mini-batch training allows operating on subgraphs, making it the standard approach for industrial-scale GNNs. Uber ATG and LinkedIn also deploy GraphSAGE-based models for recommendation and fraud detection.

whenWhy.title: Heterogeneous graphs with node attributes
whenWhy.body: GraphSAGE's aggregators (mean, pooling, LSTM) naturally handle rich node attributes without requiring a spectral graph decomposition. Unlike GCN which requires a fixed graph Laplacian, GraphSAGE can operate on heterogeneous graphs (different node/edge types) by using type-specific aggregators. HAN (Heterogeneous Attention Networks) and HetGNN extend GraphSAGE-style neighbourhood sampling to knowledge graphs and e-commerce graphs where node types (user, item, category) require different aggregation strategies.

deepDiveIntro: Hamilton, Ying and Leskovec introduced GraphSAGE with a two-step aggregation framework. For each node at each layer, the model first samples a fixed-size neighbourhood, then aggregates those neighbours' representations using a learnable function such as mean, max-pooling, or LSTM. The aggregated vector is concatenated with the node's own previous representation and passed through a linear transformation and nonlinearity. The concatenation preserves the node's own features alongside neighbourhood information — unlike GCN where self-features are averaged together with neighbours. Representations are normalised after each layer. [1]

GraphSAGE proposes four aggregators. The mean aggregator takes the element-wise mean of sampled neighbours. The GCN variant merges the self-representation into the mean without concatenation. The max-pooling aggregator captures extreme values in the neighbourhood by applying a shared MLP to each neighbour and taking the maximum. The LSTM aggregator applies an LSTM over a random permutation of neighbours to remove order bias. Max-pooling and LSTM aggregators outperform the mean aggregator on most benchmarks. [1]
formula: h_v^k = σ( W^k · [h_v^{k−1} || AGGREGATE({h_u^{k−1}, u ∈ S_{N(v)}}) ] )

deepDive.heading: Neighbourhood Sampling Strategy
deepDive.body: GraphSAGE samples K-hop neighbourhoods: for a 2-layer model, sample S_1 first-hop neighbours, then S_2 second-hop neighbours for each first-hop neighbour. Total nodes per batch: B × S_1 × S_2. Hamilton et al. Uniform sampling ignores edge importance — all neighbours equally likely regardless of edge weight or relationship strength. PinSage improves on this with importance-based sampling: run a short random walk from target node v, use visit frequencies as sampling probabilities, so closely-related nodes are sampled more often. This biased sampling improved recommendation quality over uniform GraphSAGE. PyTorch Geometric's NeighborLoader implements uniform sampling; custom importance sampling requires overriding the sampler.

deepDive.heading: Inductive vs Transductive Learning
deepDive.body: Transductive methods (GCN, DeepWalk, node2vec) learn an embedding for each node in the training graph — the embedding is a fixed lookup table that cannot generalise to new nodes. Inductive methods (GraphSAGE) learn a function that maps a node's features and neighbourhood structure to an embedding — applicable to any node. The distinction matters when: (1) new nodes join after training (social networks: new users); (2) the test graph is entirely unseen (protein function prediction on new organisms); (3) the graph is too large to train on all nodes (industrial-scale recommendation). The price: inductive methods require node features; purely structural graphs (adjacency only, no features) require hand-crafted features or degree-based features as input. GraphSAGE with degree features can recover some structural information but is less powerful than node2vec on feature-free graphs. [2]

deepDive.heading: GraphSAGE vs GCN vs GAT
deepDive.body: Aggregation comparison: GCN — fixed symmetric normalisation 1/√(d_i·d_j), no concatenation; GraphSAGE — learnable aggregator (mean/pool/LSTM), concatenation with self; GAT — attention-weighted sum, no concatenation by default. Scalability: GCN requires full graph in memory (full-batch); GAT requires full graph for attention computation; GraphSAGE supports mini-batch via neighbourhood sampling. Inductiveness: GCN and GAT are transductive (standard implementations); GraphSAGE is inductive by design. For large graphs: GraphSAGE is the baseline. For small graphs with heterogeneous neighbourhoods: GAT may be preferred. For graphs with very regular structure: GCN is computationally cheaper and competitive. On the Cora benchmark: GCN 81.5%, GAT 83.0%, GraphSAGE mean 79.8% — but comparisons are not directly informative since GraphSAGE uses mini-batch subgraph training. [2]
deepDive.formula: GCN: 1/√(d_i·d_j),  GraphSAGE: learnable agg + concat,  GAT: α_ij weighted sum

deepDive.heading: PinSage: Industrial-Scale GraphSAGE
deepDive.body: Ying et al. (2018) deployed PinSage at Pinterest on a graph with 3B nodes (pins and boards) and 18B edges. Key engineering innovations over vanilla GraphSAGE: (1) Importance sampling: uses random walk visit counts to estimate neighbourhood importance, downweighting spurious edges; (2) Curriculum training: starts with 'easy' negatives (random pins), then adds 'hard' negatives (visually similar but semantically different pins) — crucial for learning fine-grained visual distinctions; (3) Feature processing: uses VGG-16 image features for visual pins; (4) Map-reduce inference: computes node embeddings bottom-up across graph layers using distributed Hadoop, enabling inference on the 3B-node graph without loading it into memory. PinSage achieves 150% improvement in recommendations over previous collaborative filtering approach. [2]

takeaways: GraphSAGE: h_v^k = σ(W^k·[h_v^{k-1}||AGGREGATE({h_u^{k-1}, u∈S_N(v)})]); concat self + aggregated neighbours. [1]
Four aggregators: mean (GCN-like), GCN (merged mean), max-pooling (extremes), LSTM (sequential); max-pool and LSTM outperform mean on most tasks. [1]
Inductive learning: learns aggregation function, not node embeddings — generalises to unseen nodes at test time. [1]
Mini-batch via neighbourhood sampling: sample S_1 × S_2 nodes per batch; scales to billion-node graphs unlike full-batch GCN/GAT. [1]
PinSage (Pinterest): 3B nodes, 18B edges; importance sampling + curriculum training → 150% recommendation improvement. [2]

edgeCases.q: How does GraphSAGE handle nodes with no features (feature-free graphs)?
edgeCases.a: GraphSAGE requires node input features — without them, the aggregation has no meaningful signal to propagate. For feature-free graphs, common approaches: (1) Degree features: use one-hot degree encoding or scalar degree as input. Provides structural information (high-degree hubs) but limited expressiveness. (2) Random features: initialise each node with a fixed random vector, consistent across runs. This provides a unique identity without carrying semantic meaning. (3) Structural features: compute graph statistics per node (clustering coefficient, centrality, local structure counts) as input features. (4) Position encoding: spectral position encodings based on the graph Laplacian (GRIT, GraphGPS). For purely structural tasks (graph isomorphism, counting substructures), standard GraphSAGE with mean aggregation is not more expressive than the Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) graph isomorphism test — deeper expressiveness requires more powerful aggregators (GIN) or structural encodings.

edgeCases.q: What is the expressiveness limit of GraphSAGE and how does GIN address it?
edgeCases.a: Xu et al. (2019) proved that all GNNs with standard aggregators (mean, max, sum) are at most as expressive as the 1-Weisfeiler-Leman (1-WL) graph isomorphism test — meaning they cannot distinguish certain non-isomorphic graphs. Specifically: mean aggregation cannot distinguish nodes with the same mean neighbourhood feature distribution (even if the multisets differ); max pooling loses count information. The Graph Isomorphism Network (GIN) achieves the maximum possible 1-WL expressiveness by using a sum aggregator: h_v^{k} = MLP^k((1+ε)·h_v^{k-1} + Σ_{u∈N(v)} h_u^{k-1}). Sum preserves the full multiset information (unlike mean which normalises out count). GraphSAGE with mean aggregation is strictly less expressive than GIN on tasks requiring graph-level distinctions (counting triangles, bipartite detection). For node-level tasks, the practical difference is smaller.

sources.label: Hamilton, Ying & Leskovec (2017) — Inductive Representation Learning on Large Graphs (GraphSAGE), NeurIPS 2017 arXiv:1706.02216
Ying et al. (2018) — Graph Convolutional Neural Networks for Web-Scale Recommender Systems (PinSage), KDD 2018 arXiv:1806.01973
